facts.text: Roses are one of the oldest cultivated flowers, with fossil evidence suggesting they existed over 35 million years ago.
Tomatoes were once considered poisonous in Europe because they belong to the nightshade family.
Basil is one of the easiest herbs to grow and thrives in warm, sunny conditions with well-drained soil.
The USDA Plant Hardiness Zone Map divides North America into 13 zones based on average annual minimum winter temperatures.
Clay soil holds moisture well but drains poorly, making it ideal for plants like asters, bee balm, and black-eyed Susans.
Composting can reduce household waste by up to 30% while creating nutrient-rich fertilizer for your garden.
A sharp pair of bypass pruners is considered the single most essential hand tool for any gardener.
Ladybugs are one of the most beneficial garden insects, with a single ladybug capable of eating up to 5,000 aphids in its lifetime.
The Gardens of Versailles cover about 2,000 acres and took over 40 years to complete under the direction of landscape architect André Le Nôtre.
Raised bed gardening allows you to control soil quality and drainage, making it easier to grow plants in areas with poor native soil.
Sunflowers are heliotropic when young, meaning they follow the sun across the sky from east to west each day.
Peppers and tomatoes are both members of the Solanaceae family, commonly called the nightshade family.
Mint is an extremely vigorous grower and should be planted in containers to prevent it from taking over your garden.
USDA Zone 10 covers areas like southern Florida and coastal Southern California, where tropical plants thrive year-round.
Sandy soil drains quickly and warms up fast in spring, but it struggles to retain nutrients and moisture.
Vermicomposting uses red wiggler worms to break down kitchen scraps into rich, dark compost called worm castings.
A garden hoe is one of the oldest farming tools, dating back thousands of years and still essential for weeding.
Companion planting marigolds near tomatoes can help repel nematodes and whiteflies naturally.
Kew Gardens in London houses over 50,000 living plants and has been a UNESCO World Heritage Site since 2003.
Container gardening is ideal for small spaces like balconies and patios, allowing anyone to grow herbs, flowers, and even vegetables.
Tulips were once so valuable in the Netherlands during the 1630s that single bulbs sold for more than the price of a house.
Cucumbers are 95% water, making them one of the most hydrating vegetables you can grow in your garden.
Rosemary is a Mediterranean herb that prefers dry, well-drained soil and can tolerate drought once established.
Zone 5, covering much of the upper Midwest and New England, has minimum winter temperatures of -20°F to -10°F.
Loam soil is considered the ideal garden soil because it contains a balanced mixture of sand, silt, and clay.
Hot composting reaches temperatures of 130-160°F, which kills most weed seeds and pathogens in the pile.
Garden rakes come in two main types: bow rakes for moving soil and leaf rakes for gathering lightweight debris.
Neem oil is an organic pesticide derived from the neem tree that controls over 200 species of insects.
Butchart Gardens in British Columbia, Canada, was created in a former limestone quarry and attracts over a million visitors annually.
Square foot gardening divides raised beds into one-foot squares, each planted with a specific number of plants based on their size.
Lavender is drought-tolerant once established and attracts pollinators like bees and butterflies to the garden.
Squash plants produce separate male and female flowers on the same vine, and bees are essential for pollination.
Thyme is a low-growing herb that makes an excellent ground cover and releases a pleasant fragrance when stepped on.
USDA Zone 7 covers much of the mid-Atlantic and parts of the Pacific Northwest, with winter lows of 0°F to 10°F.
Peat soil is acidic and moisture-retentive, making it suitable for acid-loving plants like blueberries and azaleas.
Compost tea is made by steeping finished compost in water and can be used as a liquid fertilizer or foliar spray.
A wheelbarrow is indispensable for moving heavy loads of soil, compost, mulch, and garden debris.
Diatomaceous earth is a non-toxic powder made from fossilized algae that kills soft-bodied insects by dehydrating them.
Keukenhof in the Netherlands plants approximately 7 million flower bulbs each year and is known as the Garden of Europe.
Hydroponics is a method of growing plants without soil, using nutrient-rich water solutions instead.
Orchids are one of the largest families of flowering plants, with over 25,000 species found on every continent except Antarctica.
Zucchini is one of the most prolific garden vegetables; a single plant can produce 6 to 10 pounds of fruit per season.
Cilantro bolts quickly in hot weather, so it's best planted in cool seasons or in partial shade during summer.
Zone 3 gardens experience winter temperatures as low as -40°F, limiting plant choices to extremely cold-hardy species.
Adding organic matter like compost to clay soil improves its drainage and makes it easier for plant roots to penetrate.
A three-bin composting system allows you to have compost at different stages of decomposition at all times.
Long-handled loppers provide the leverage needed to cut branches up to 2 inches in diameter.
Planting garlic near roses can help deter aphids and Japanese beetles without chemical pesticides.
The Hanging Gardens of Babylon are one of the Seven Wonders of the Ancient World, though their exact location remains debated.
Vertical gardening uses trellises, wall planters, and stacked containers to maximize growing space in small areas.
Daisies belong to the Asteraceae family, one of the largest plant families with over 32,000 known species.
Carrots come in many colors besides orange, including purple, red, yellow, and white varieties.
Parsley is a biennial herb that produces leaves in its first year and flowers and seeds in its second year.
Zone 9 covers much of the Gulf Coast and parts of Arizona, where gardeners can grow citrus trees and tropical plants.
Adding sand to clay soil without enough organic matter can actually create a concrete-like mixture that's worse than the original clay.
Bokashi composting is an anaerobic fermentation method that can process meat and dairy scraps that traditional composting cannot.
A soil knife, or hori-hori, is a versatile Japanese gardening tool used for digging, cutting, weeding, and transplanting.
Beneficial insects like green lacewings prey on aphids, mealybugs, and other common garden pests.
The Royal Botanic Garden Edinburgh was founded in 1670 and is one of the oldest scientific gardens in the world.
Straw bale gardening involves conditioning bales with water and fertilizer, then planting directly into the decomposing straw.
Pothos is one of the most forgiving houseplants, tolerating low light, irregular watering, and a variety of soil conditions.
Beans and peas are nitrogen-fixing plants that convert atmospheric nitrogen into a form usable by other plants.
Sage is a perennial herb that develops woody stems over time and can live for many years in the right conditions.
Microclimates within your yard can vary by a full zone or more, allowing you to grow plants not typically suited to your area.
Gypsum can be added to sodic soils to improve structure by replacing sodium ions with calcium ions on clay particles.
Carbon-to-nitrogen ratio in composting should ideally be about 30:1 for efficient decomposition.
A garden fork is better than a shovel for turning compost and loosening compacted soil without disrupting soil structure.
Praying mantises are generalist predators in the garden that eat both beneficial and pest insects.
Giverny, Claude Monet's garden in France, was the inspiration for many of his famous water lily paintings.
Aquaponics combines hydroponics with fish farming, using fish waste as natural fertilizer for the plants.
Snake plants (Sansevieria) are among the best air-purifying houseplants and can survive in very low light conditions.
Lettuce prefers cool weather and can bolt and become bitter when temperatures consistently exceed 75°F.
Chives are one of the hardiest herbs, returning year after year in zones 3 through 9 with minimal care.
Zone 6 has minimum winter temperatures of -10°F to 0°F and supports a wide variety of perennials and fruit trees.
Perlite is a volcanic glass that is added to potting soil to improve drainage and aeration for container plants.
Grass clippings left on the lawn after mowing return nitrogen and other nutrients back to the soil naturally.
A broadfork is a manual tool used to aerate soil deeply without turning it over, preserving soil structure and microorganisms.
Floating row covers protect plants from frost and insects while allowing light, air, and water to reach the plants.
Sissinghurst Castle Garden in England is famous for its white garden, designed by Vita Sackville-West in the 1930s.
Lasagna gardening, or sheet mulching, layers organic materials directly on the ground to create a no-dig garden bed.
Fiddle leaf figs need bright, indirect light and are sensitive to drafts, overwatering, and being moved frequently.
Strawberries can be grown in hanging baskets, towers, or traditional garden beds and produce fruit within the first year.
Dill attracts beneficial insects like parasitic wasps and hoverflies that prey on aphids and caterpillars.
Zone 8 covers the Pacific Northwest coast and much of the Southeast, with winter lows of 10°F to 20°F.
Vermiculite retains moisture in soil mixes and is often used alongside perlite for seed-starting mixes.
Mushroom compost, a byproduct of mushroom farming, is rich in nutrients but can be too alkaline for acid-loving plants.
Oscillating hoes, also called stirrup hoes, cut weeds just below the soil surface on both the push and pull stroke.
Bacillus thuringiensis (Bt) is a naturally occurring bacterium used as a biological pesticide against caterpillars and mosquitoes.
The Alhambra gardens in Granada, Spain, feature intricate Islamic-style water features and geometric plantings dating to the 13th century.
Keyhole gardens are circular raised beds with a composting basket in the center, popular in arid climates for water efficiency.
Succulents store water in their leaves, stems, or roots, making them extremely drought-tolerant and low-maintenance houseplants.
Garlic is typically planted in the fall, overwinters in the ground, and is harvested the following summer.
Lemon balm is a vigorous self-seeder in the mint family that can become invasive if not regularly deadheaded.
The first USDA Plant Hardiness Zone Map was published in 1960 and has been updated several times since.
Cover crops like clover, rye, and vetch protect soil from erosion and add nutrients when tilled under in spring.
Cold composting is as simple as piling organic materials and letting them decompose naturally over 6 to 24 months.
A dibber is a pointed tool used to make holes in soil for planting seeds, seedlings, and bulbs.
Copper tape and barriers can deter slugs and snails from reaching plants without using chemical baits.
The Montreal Botanical Garden is one of the largest in the world, covering 190 acres with 22,000 plant species.
Wicking beds use a water reservoir below the soil that delivers moisture upward through capillary action, reducing watering frequency.
Spider plants are easy-to-grow houseplants that produce baby plants on long runners, making propagation simple.
Sweet potatoes need a long, warm growing season of at least 90 to 170 days and thrive in loose, sandy soil.
Bay laurel is a slow-growing evergreen tree whose leaves are used in cooking and can be grown in containers.
Zone 4 gardens experience winter temperatures of -30°F to -20°F and can grow hardy perennials like hostas and daylilies.
Biochar is a charcoal-like substance added to soil that improves water retention, nutrient holding, and microbial activity.
Leaf mold, made from decomposed leaves, is an excellent soil conditioner that improves moisture retention.
A Warren hoe has a pointed, triangular blade ideal for making furrows and planting rows in the garden.
Introducing parasitic wasps to your garden can control whitefly, aphid, and caterpillar populations without harming other insects.
The Desert Botanical Garden in Phoenix, Arizona, showcases over 50,000 desert plants including cacti from around the world.
Hügelkultur is a permaculture technique that creates raised beds from buried logs and branches, which decompose and retain moisture.
Peace lilies are popular indoor plants that can bloom in low light and help remove toxins from indoor air.
Beets are a cool-season root vegetable that can be planted in early spring or late summer for a fall harvest.
Oregano is a perennial herb in warm climates and an annual in cold zones, preferring full sun and lean soil.
Zone 11 has no frost and covers tropical areas like Hawaii and Key West, where exotic fruits and flowers flourish.
Sulfur can be added to alkaline soil to lower pH, making nutrients more available to acid-loving plants.
Trench composting involves burying kitchen scraps directly in garden trenches, where they decompose in place over several months.
A hand trowel is one of the most frequently used garden tools, essential for digging small holes and transplanting seedlings.
Beer traps are a simple, non-toxic method for attracting and drowning slugs in the garden.
Longwood Gardens in Pennsylvania features 1,077 acres of gardens, meadows, and woodlands with spectacular fountain displays.
Espalier is an ancient technique of training trees to grow flat against a wall or trellis, saving space and improving fruit production.
Aloe vera is a succulent houseplant known for its medicinal gel and needs bright light with infrequent watering.
Pumpkins need a lot of space, with vines spreading 10 to 20 feet, and require a long growing season of 75 to 100 days.
Tarragon comes in French and Russian varieties; French tarragon has superior flavor but cannot be grown from seed.
The heat island effect in cities can shift the local USDA zone by one or even two zones warmer than surrounding areas.
Lime is added to acidic soil to raise the pH, making nutrients like phosphorus and calcium more available to plants.
Worm bins can be kept indoors and process food scraps year-round, producing compost and liquid fertilizer.
Anvil pruners crush stems between a blade and a flat surface and are best used for dead or woody branches.
Chrysanthemums contain pyrethrin, a natural insecticide, and can help repel ants, roaches, and ticks from the garden.
The Garden of Cosmic Speculation in Scotland features mathematical and scientific concepts reflected in its landscape design.
French intensive gardening uses deeply dug, richly amended beds to grow plants very closely together for maximum yield.
ZZ plants (Zamioculcas zamiifolia) tolerate neglect, low light, and infrequent watering, making them ideal for beginners.
Radishes are one of the fastest-growing vegetables, ready to harvest in as few as 25 days from planting.
Fennel can inhibit the growth of nearby plants, so it is often planted on its own or at the edges of the garden.
The AHS Plant Heat Zone Map measures heat tolerance using the number of days above 86°F per year.
Green sand, derived from marine sediment, is an organic amendment that adds potassium, iron, and trace minerals to soil.
Compost activators containing nitrogen can speed up the decomposition of carbon-heavy materials like dried leaves and straw.
A soil pH meter is an inexpensive tool that helps gardeners quickly determine whether their soil is acidic, neutral, or alkaline.
Releasing beneficial nematodes into the soil can control grub, flea, and fungus gnat larvae organically.
The Boboli Gardens in Florence, Italy, are one of the earliest examples of Italian Renaissance garden design, dating to the 16th century.
Succession planting involves sowing seeds at regular intervals to ensure a continuous harvest throughout the growing season.
Rubber plants (Ficus elastica) are low-maintenance houseplants that can grow into impressive indoor trees with proper care.
Eggplant needs warm soil to germinate and should not be transplanted outdoors until night temperatures stay above 55°F.
Borage is an herb with edible blue flowers that attracts bees and is considered an excellent companion plant for tomatoes and strawberries.
Maritime climates like the Pacific Northwest have mild winters and cool summers, supporting unique plant palettes.
Wood ash from untreated wood can be added to garden soil to raise pH and supply potassium and calcium.
A simple compost pile needs just four ingredients: greens (nitrogen), browns (carbon), water, and air.
Soaker hoses deliver water directly to the base of plants, reducing water waste from evaporation and runoff by up to 70%.
Crop rotation prevents the buildup of soil-borne diseases and pests by changing what is planted in each area each year.
The Jardin Majorelle in Marrakech, Morocco, features cobalt blue buildings surrounded by exotic plants from five continents.
Biointensive gardening focuses on maximizing yield from a small area while maintaining soil fertility through composting and close spacing.
Chinese evergreens (Aglaonema) are colorful, low-light houseplants that tolerate neglect and poor air quality.
Kale is one of the hardiest greens, surviving temperatures down to 10°F and actually becoming sweeter after a frost.
Chamomile is known as the 'plant doctor' because it seems to help ailing plants recover when planted nearby.
Aspect and elevation within a garden create microclimates that can be exploited to grow a wider range of plants.
Bone meal is an organic fertilizer high in phosphorus that promotes strong root development and flowering.
Grasscycling — leaving clippings on the lawn — can provide up to 25% of a lawn's annual fertilizer needs.
A garden kneeler with handles doubles as a seat and reduces knee and back strain during planting tasks.
Insecticidal soap made from potassium salts of fatty acids is effective against soft-bodied insects like aphids and spider mites.
The Butchart Gardens in British Columbia bloom continuously from March through October with seasonal floral displays.
No-till gardening preserves soil structure and microbial communities by avoiding digging or turning the soil.
Boston ferns are popular hanging houseplants that thrive in humid environments with indirect light.
Watermelons need 70 to 90 days of warm weather and plenty of space, as their vines can spread 15 to 20 feet.
Lemongrass is a tropical herb used in Asian cooking that also acts as a natural mosquito repellent in the garden.
Rain gardens are shallow depressions planted with native species that capture and filter stormwater runoff.
Blood meal is a high-nitrogen organic fertilizer that promotes leafy green growth and can also deter deer from the garden.
The ideal compost moisture level is like a wrung-out sponge — damp but not dripping.
A quality garden hose should be at least 5/8 inch in diameter for adequate water flow to most garden tasks.
Trap crops like nasturtiums lure aphids away from more valuable plants, acting as sacrificial decoys.
The Singapore Botanic Gardens became Singapore's first UNESCO World Heritage Site in 2015.
Permaculture garden design works with natural ecosystems to create self-sustaining, productive landscapes.
Philodendrons are versatile houseplants available in climbing and non-climbing varieties that thrive in medium to bright indirect light.
Brussels sprouts need a long growing season of 80 to 100 days and taste best after being touched by frost.
Lovage is a tall, perennial herb with a celery-like flavor that can grow up to 6 feet tall.
South-facing slopes receive the most sun in the Northern Hemisphere, making them the warmest microclimates in a yard.
Fish emulsion is a fast-acting organic fertilizer that provides nitrogen, phosphorus, and trace minerals to plants.
Turning a compost pile every few weeks introduces oxygen that accelerates the breakdown of organic materials.
Drip irrigation delivers water slowly and directly to plant roots, reducing water usage by 30 to 50% compared to sprinklers.
Interplanting fast-growing crops like lettuce between slow-growing ones like broccoli maximizes garden space.
The Huntington Botanical Gardens in California house one of the world's largest collections of cacti and succulents.
Food forests mimic natural woodland ecosystems with layers of canopy trees, understory trees, shrubs, herbs, and ground covers.
Monstera deliciosa, the Swiss cheese plant, has become one of the most popular houseplants due to its dramatic fenestrated leaves.
Asparagus is a perennial vegetable that can produce harvests for 15 to 20 years once established.
Marjoram is closely related to oregano but has a sweeter, milder flavor and is less cold-hardy.
The first day of frost in fall and the last frost date in spring define the length of the local growing season.
Kelp meal is a slow-release organic fertilizer that provides over 60 trace minerals and natural growth hormones to plants.
A well-managed compost bin can convert kitchen and yard waste into usable compost in as little as 3 to 4 weeks.
Mulching around plants with 2 to 4 inches of organic material suppresses weeds, retains moisture, and regulates soil temperature.
Beneficial ground beetles are nocturnal predators that eat slugs, snails, cutworms, and other garden pests.
The Palace of Versailles gardens feature 400 sculptures, 1,400 fountains, and 200,000 trees across the grounds.
Companion planting basil with tomatoes is believed to improve tomato flavor and repel flies and mosquitoes.
Jade plants are popular succulents that can live for decades and are considered symbols of good luck.
Corn is a wind-pollinated crop that should be planted in blocks of at least four rows rather than single long rows.
Stevia is a natural sweetener herb that can be grown in the garden and is 200 to 300 times sweeter than sugar.
Frost pockets form in low-lying areas where cold air settles, making them several degrees colder than surrounding elevations.
Epsom salt (magnesium sulfate) can be used as a soil amendment to correct magnesium deficiency in plants.
The carbon-rich 'brown' materials for composting include dried leaves, straw, cardboard, and wood chips.
A rain gauge helps gardeners determine how much supplemental watering their garden needs each week.
Milky spore disease is a biological control that kills Japanese beetle grubs in lawns without harming other organisms.
The Château de Villandry in France is famous for its ornamental vegetable gardens arranged in geometric patterns.
Biodynamic gardening follows a planting calendar based on lunar and planetary cycles and emphasizes holistic farm health.
Calathea plants are prized houseplants known for their colorful, patterned leaves that fold up at night.
Onions are classified by day length: short-day varieties for southern regions and long-day varieties for northern areas.
Bee balm (Monarda) is a native perennial that attracts hummingbirds, bees, and butterflies with its showy flowers.
Wind protection from hedges or fences can extend the growing season by raising local temperatures and reducing wind chill damage.
Cottonseed meal is an organic fertilizer that is mildly acidic, making it beneficial for acid-loving plants like azaleas and blueberries.
The nitrogen-rich 'green' materials for composting include fresh grass clippings, fruit scraps, vegetable peels, and coffee grounds.
Self-watering planters have built-in reservoirs that provide consistent moisture to plants through capillary action.
Attracting solitary bees by providing nesting sites like bee hotels can significantly increase pollination in home gardens.
Monet's garden at Giverny includes a water garden with Japanese bridges and the famous water lily pond.
The Three Sisters planting technique, used by Indigenous peoples, grows corn, beans, and squash together in a symbiotic arrangement.
English ivy is a vigorous climber that can become invasive outdoors but is a useful trailing houseplant indoors.
Potatoes can be grown in bags, barrels, or stacked tires, making them suitable for small-space gardening.
Sorrel is a perennial herb with a tangy, lemony flavor that is one of the first greens available in spring.
Trees planted on the south and west sides of a home can reduce summer cooling costs by up to 25%.
Rock phosphate is a natural, slow-release source of phosphorus that becomes more available in acidic soils.
Compost should smell earthy, not foul; a bad smell usually indicates too much nitrogen or insufficient aeration.
Raised beds should be no wider than 4 feet so you can reach the center from either side without stepping on the soil.
Hover flies, which look like small bees, are important pollinators whose larvae eat large numbers of aphids.
The Ryoan-ji Temple in Kyoto, Japan, features one of the most famous zen rock gardens, containing 15 carefully placed stones.
Companion planting nasturtiums near cucumbers can repel cucumber beetles and other pests.
Cast iron plants (Aspidistra elatior) earned their name because they can survive extreme neglect and very low light.
Celery requires consistently moist soil and a long growing season of about 130 to 140 days from seed to harvest.
Lavender prefers alkaline soil and can suffer root rot in heavy, wet clay soils without proper drainage.
The length of daylight hours, or photoperiod, influences when many plants flower, go dormant, or set fruit.
Alfalfa meal is an organic amendment that provides nitrogen, phosphorus, and triacontanol, a natural growth stimulant.
Adding coffee grounds directly to garden soil can improve drainage and attract earthworms that aerate the soil.
Timer-controlled drip irrigation systems ensure consistent watering even when gardeners are away from home.
Lacewing larvae, sometimes called aphid lions, are voracious predators that can eat 200 aphids per week.
The Powerscourt Gardens in Ireland are among the finest examples of an Italianate garden in the British Isles.
Double-digging involves loosening soil to a depth of 24 inches, improving root penetration and drainage in compacted areas.
Pothos (Epipremnum aureum) can grow in water alone, making it an extremely easy plant to propagate and share.
Turnips are a fast-maturing root vegetable that can go from seed to harvest in just 30 to 60 days.
Winter savory is a perennial herb while summer savory is an annual; both have a peppery flavor used in bean dishes.
Zone 2 is one of the coldest zones in the USDA map, with winter lows of -50°F to -40°F, found in northern Canada and Alaska.
Greensand is a mineral-rich soil amendment mined from ancient ocean beds that provides potassium and iron.
Finished compost should be dark, crumbly, and have an earthy smell with no recognizable food scraps remaining.
Landscape fabric placed under mulch can suppress weeds but may restrict water and air movement if used improperly.
Trichogramma wasps are tiny parasitoids that lay their eggs inside pest insect eggs, preventing them from hatching.
The Brooklyn Botanic Garden in New York features a Japanese Hill-and-Pond Garden created in 1914.
Polyculture planting — growing multiple crop species together — can reduce pest problems and increase overall yields.
Dracaena marginata (dragon tree) is a striking houseplant with long, narrow leaves edged in red or pink.
Swiss chard is a colorful, heat-tolerant green that can be harvested continuously by picking outer leaves first.
Catnip is easy to grow and repels mosquitoes, but will attract neighborhood cats to your garden.
Frost blankets and cold frames extend the growing season by protecting plants from light freezes in spring and fall.
Worm castings contain beneficial microorganisms that help suppress soil-borne diseases and improve plant health.
The oldest known garden plan is a map of an Egyptian garden from around 1400 BC, found in a tomb in Thebes.
A soil test from your local cooperative extension office can reveal pH, nutrient levels, and amendment recommendations for your garden.
Assassin bugs are beneficial predators that feed on caterpillars, beetles, and other pest insects in the garden.
The Generalife gardens of the Alhambra in Granada were designed as a retreat for the Moorish kings.
Window boxes are a form of container gardening that add color and plants to homes without yard space.
Croton plants have brilliantly colored leaves in red, orange, and yellow but need bright light to maintain their color.
Pole beans can grow 6 to 10 feet tall and produce more beans per square foot than bush bean varieties.
Angelica is a dramatic biennial herb that can reach 6 feet tall and has been used in herbal medicine for centuries.
Zone 12 and 13 are the warmest USDA zones, found only in tropical regions like Puerto Rico and Hawaii.
Humus is the dark, stable form of decomposed organic matter that gives healthy soil its rich color and water-holding capacity.
Tumbler composters are enclosed bins that can be rotated to mix compost easily, speeding up the decomposition process.
A garden auger attachment for a drill makes quick work of planting dozens of bulbs in fall.
Planting flowering herbs like dill, fennel, and cilantro attracts beneficial insects that help control garden pests.
The National Garden of Athens is a public park established in 1838 that covers 38 acres in the heart of the city.
Mittleider gardening uses custom soil-less growing mixes in narrow raised beds with precise fertilizer formulas.
Norfolk Island pines are popular indoor conifers often used as tabletop Christmas trees in tropical and subtropical climates.
Kohlrabi is an unusual brassica vegetable that forms a swollen stem above ground and tastes like a mild, sweet turnip.
Tansy was historically used as a companion plant to repel ants, flies, and beetles from the garden.
Spring-blooming bulbs like tulips, daffodils, and crocuses must be planted in fall to receive the cold period they need to bloom.
Mycorrhizal fungi form symbiotic relationships with plant roots, extending their reach for water and nutrients by up to 700 times.
Fall leaves collected and shredded make excellent free mulch and compost material for the garden.
Ollas are unglazed clay pots buried in the soil that slowly release water to plant roots, an ancient irrigation method from North Africa.
Damsel bugs are generalist predators that feed on aphids, small caterpillars, leafhoppers, and thrips.
The Villa d'Este gardens near Rome, Italy, are famous for their terraced hillside and hundreds of Renaissance-era fountains.
Back-to-Eden gardening covers the ground with a thick layer of wood chips that mimic a forest floor.
Air plants (Tillandsia) absorb water and nutrients through their leaves and don't need soil to grow.
Artichokes are actually large thistle buds that are harvested before they bloom and can be perennial in mild climates.
Horseradish is a vigorous perennial root that can become invasive and is best grown in a contained area.
The Royal Horticultural Society's Chelsea Flower Show has been held annually since 1913 and is the world's most prestigious garden show.
Silt soil has fine particles and good fertility but can become compacted easily and may drain poorly without amendments.
Mushroom compost is an excellent mulch and soil conditioner but should be used sparingly due to its high salt content.
A compost thermometer helps monitor internal pile temperatures to ensure efficient hot composting.
Soldier beetles are beneficial garden insects that eat aphids and also serve as pollinators for flowers.
The Jardin des Plantes in Paris was originally established in 1626 as a royal medicinal herb garden.
Biodegradable seed-starting pots made from peat or cow manure can be planted directly in the ground to reduce transplant shock.
Dumb cane (Dieffenbachia) is a popular houseplant with large, patterned leaves but is toxic if ingested by pets or children.
Okra is a heat-loving vegetable that thrives in temperatures above 85°F and is a staple of Southern cooking.
Hyssop is a semi-evergreen herb with blue-violet flower spikes that attract bees and butterflies throughout summer.
Wind tunnels formed between buildings can dry out plants quickly and may require more frequent watering and wind-tolerant species.
Composting leaves separately creates leaf mold, which is primarily a soil conditioner rather than a fertilizer.
Victory gardens during World War II produced an estimated 40% of the fresh vegetables consumed in the United States.
A stirrup hoe is one of the most efficient tools for quickly weeding between rows of plants in a vegetable garden.
Robber flies are predatory insects that catch and eat other flying insects, including many common garden pests.
The Nong Nooch Tropical Garden in Thailand covers 500 acres and features themed gardens representing different countries.
Chinampas are floating garden beds used by the Aztecs, created by layering vegetation on shallow lake beds.
Schefflera (umbrella plant) is a tropical houseplant that can grow into a small indoor tree with glossy, compound leaves.
Parsnips require a long growing season and taste sweetest after being left in the ground through fall frosts.
Yarrow is a drought-tolerant perennial herb whose flowers attract beneficial insects like ladybugs and hoverflies.
Heat-loving vegetables like tomatoes, peppers, and eggplant should not be transplanted outdoors until soil temperatures reach at least 60°F.
Azomite is a mineral-rich rock dust that provides over 70 trace minerals and elements to garden soil.
Anaerobic composting occurs in sealed containers without air and produces compost through a fermentation process.
A quality pair of gardening gloves protects hands from thorns, blisters, and soil-borne pathogens while working.
Syrphid flies, also called flower flies, look like tiny wasps but are harmless pollinators whose larvae consume aphids.
The Claude Monet Foundation maintains Giverny's gardens exactly as the artist designed them, attracting 500,000 visitors annually.
Ruth Stout pioneered the deep mulch gardening method in the 1950s, covering her entire garden with a thick layer of spoiled hay.
Pilea peperomioides, the Chinese money plant, became a viral sensation due to its cute, round coin-shaped leaves.
Rhubarb is a perennial vegetable grown for its tart stalks; its leaves are toxic due to high oxalic acid content.
Chervil is a delicate herb with an anise-like flavor that grows best in partial shade and cool weather.
A south-facing wall can act as a thermal mass in the garden, absorbing heat during the day and radiating it at night.
Rock dust or glacial rock dust is a slow-release mineral amendment that replenishes trace elements depleted from soil.
The Berkeley hot composting method can produce finished compost in as little as 18 days using frequent turning and precise ratios.
A wheel hoe allows gardeners to cultivate and weed between rows quickly while standing, reducing back strain.
Mason bees are solitary, non-aggressive bees that are more efficient pollinators per bee than honeybees.
The Suzhou classical gardens in China are UNESCO World Heritage Sites representing the pinnacle of Chinese garden design.
Edible landscaping replaces ornamental plants with food-producing ones, combining beauty with practicality.
String of pearls (Senecio rowleyanus) is a trailing succulent with bead-like leaves that looks stunning in hanging baskets.
Tomatillos are related to tomatoes and need at least two plants for cross-pollination to produce fruit.
Caraway is a biennial herb whose seeds are widely used in European baking and whose roots are also edible.
The concept of hardiness zones was developed by the Arnold Arboretum of Harvard University in the 1920s.
Soil organic matter should ideally comprise 3% to 5% of garden soil for optimal plant growth and microbial activity.
Static composting bins use passive aeration through ventilation holes, requiring less labor but taking longer to produce compost.
A mattock combines an axe blade and an adze for breaking up hard soil, cutting roots, and removing rocks from garden beds.
Minute pirate bugs are tiny predators that attack thrips, spider mites, aphids, and insect eggs in the garden.
Stourhead in Wiltshire, England, is considered one of the finest examples of an 18th-century English landscape garden.
Spiral herb gardens plant herbs in a corkscrew shape that creates multiple microclimates for different water and sun needs.
The parlor palm (Chamaedorea elegans) is one of the few palms that thrives in low indoor light conditions.
Turnip greens are more nutritious than the roots themselves and are popular in Southern cuisine.
Elderberries are easy-to-grow shrubs that produce berries used in syrups, wines, and immune-boosting preparations.
A garden journal tracking planting dates, varieties, weather, and yields helps gardeners improve results year after year.
Coir (coconut fiber) is a sustainable alternative to peat moss for seed starting and soil amendment.
In-vessel composting systems are enclosed and can process large volumes of organic waste rapidly in controlled conditions.
A bulb planter makes quick, uniform holes for planting spring and fall bulbs at the proper depth.
Big-eyed bugs are generalist predators that eat aphids, caterpillar eggs, mites, and whiteflies.
The Rikugien Garden in Tokyo is a classic Edo-period strolling garden featuring seasonal displays of wisteria and autumn foliage.
Forest gardening arranges edible plants in layers that mimic a natural forest, from tall canopy trees to ground covers.
Rubber tree plants can grow up to 10 feet indoors and are one of the best indoor trees for beginners.
Celeriac is a knobby root vegetable related to celery that has a nutty, celery-like flavor and stores well through winter.
Savory is known as the bean herb in Germany because it pairs perfectly with all types of beans.
The first known vegetable garden in North America was planted by European colonists in Jamestown, Virginia in 1607.
Hugelkultur beds can retain enough moisture to eliminate the need for irrigation after the first year.
Adding too much nitrogen fertilizer causes excessive leafy growth at the expense of flowers and fruit production.
Dibbles with depth markings help ensure seeds and bulbs are planted at the correct depth for optimal germination.
Tachinid flies parasitize caterpillars, beetles, and stink bugs, making them valuable biological pest control agents.
Bodnant Garden in Wales is famous for its laburnum arch, a 180-foot tunnel of golden hanging flowers in late spring.
Mandala gardens are circular designs inspired by sacred geometry that promote biodiversity and efficient space use.
Hoya plants, known as wax plants, produce fragrant, star-shaped flower clusters and thrive as trailing or climbing houseplants.
Kohlrabi can go from seed to harvest in just 45 to 60 days, making it a great fast-growing cool-season crop.
The honeybee visits between 50 and 1,000 flowers per foraging trip and is essential for pollinating many food crops.
Hardening off seedlings by gradually exposing them to outdoor conditions over 7 to 10 days prevents transplant shock.
Vermiculite is a mineral that is heated until it expands, creating a lightweight additive that retains moisture in potting mixes.
A compost sieve or screen separates finished compost from larger, undecomposed materials that need more time.
Rove beetles are active hunters in the garden that eat root maggots, aphids, and other soil-dwelling pests.
The Isola Bella gardens on Lake Maggiore in Italy feature 10 terraces rising 120 feet above the lake in a baroque style.
Keyhole gardening originated in Africa as a water-efficient technique for growing vegetables in hot, dry climates.
Sago palms are not true palms but cycads, and are extremely toxic to pets if any part of the plant is ingested.
Salsify is an heirloom root vegetable sometimes called the oyster plant because of its mild oyster-like flavor when cooked.
Cutting gardens are planted specifically to provide flowers for indoor bouquets without depleting landscape displays.
Gertrude Jekyll was a pioneering English garden designer who created over 400 gardens and popularized the herbaceous border.
Capability Brown, known as England's greatest gardener, designed over 170 landscape gardens in the 18th century.
The oldest known cultivated garden in the world may be the garden at Pasargadae in Iran, dating to around 500 BC.
Luther Burbank developed over 800 plant varieties during his career, including the Shasta daisy and the Burbank potato.
pH stands for 'potential of hydrogen' and is measured on a scale of 0 to 14, with 7 being neutral.
Most garden vegetables grow best in slightly acidic soil with a pH between 6.0 and 7.0.
Blueberries require very acidic soil with a pH of 4.5 to 5.5, much lower than most garden plants.
Butterflies prefer flat-topped flowers like zinnias, lantana, and coneflowers where they can easily land and feed.
Hummingbirds are attracted to red and tubular flowers like salvia, trumpet vine, and fuchsia.
The monarch butterfly depends on milkweed plants as the sole food source for its caterpillars.
Native plants typically require less water, fertilizer, and pest control than non-native ornamental species.
Xeriscaping is a landscaping approach that reduces or eliminates the need for supplemental water from irrigation.
A rain barrel connected to a downspout can collect 1,300 gallons of water from just 1 inch of rain on a 2,000 square foot roof.
Overwatering is the number one killer of houseplants, causing root rot and fungal diseases.
The best time to water outdoor plants is early morning, which reduces evaporation and allows foliage to dry before nightfall.
Deep, infrequent watering encourages roots to grow deeper into the soil, making plants more drought-resistant.
Greywater from sinks and showers can be safely used to irrigate ornamental gardens if biodegradable soaps are used.
Lawn grass typically needs about 1 inch of water per week, whether from rainfall or irrigation.
Dethatching removes the layer of dead grass and roots that builds up between the soil surface and living grass blades.
Aerating a lawn by pulling small plugs of soil improves water infiltration, reduces compaction, and promotes healthier grass.
Cool-season grasses like fescue and bluegrass grow best in temperatures of 60°F to 75°F and go dormant in summer heat.
Warm-season grasses like Bermuda, zoysia, and St. Augustine thrive in temperatures of 80°F to 95°F.
Mowing at the highest recommended setting for your grass type shades the soil, reduces water loss, and discourages weeds.
Clover was once a standard component of lawn seed mixes because it fixes nitrogen and stays green during drought.
Seed starting indoors under grow lights gives gardeners a 6 to 8 week head start on the growing season.
Bottom watering seed trays promotes stronger root growth as roots grow downward toward the water source.
Damping off is a fungal disease that kills seedlings at the soil line, often caused by overwatering and poor air circulation.
Stratification is the process of exposing seeds to cold, moist conditions to break dormancy, mimicking a natural winter.
Scarification involves nicking or sanding hard seed coats to allow water to penetrate and trigger germination.
Some seeds, like lettuce and chamomile, need light to germinate and should be pressed onto the soil surface rather than buried.
Stem cuttings placed in water or moist soil can generate new roots and grow into clones of the parent plant.
Division is the simplest propagation method for perennials, involving splitting an established plant into two or more sections.
Layering encourages roots to form on a stem still attached to the parent plant, then severing it once rooted.
Grafting joins the top portion (scion) of one plant to the rootstock of another, combining the best traits of both.
Hardwood cuttings taken from dormant deciduous shrubs in late fall can be rooted in sand over winter.
The Fibonacci spiral appears frequently in plant growth patterns, including sunflower seed heads and pine cones.
Photosynthesis converts carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen using energy from sunlight in plant leaves.
Plants absorb most of their nutrients through root hairs, tiny extensions of root cells that increase surface area.
Nitrogen promotes leafy green growth, phosphorus supports roots and flowers, and potassium enhances overall plant vigor.
Secondary macronutrients calcium, magnesium, and sulfur are needed in smaller amounts but are still essential for plant health.
Iron deficiency in plants causes chlorosis, yellowing between leaf veins while the veins remain green.
Deadheading spent flowers redirects a plant's energy from seed production back into producing more blooms.
Pinching the growing tips of herbs like basil encourages bushier growth and prevents premature flowering.
Hardening off transplants involves gradually increasing their exposure to outdoor conditions over 7 to 14 days.
Determinate tomatoes grow to a set size and produce all fruit at once, while indeterminate varieties produce continuously until frost.
Heirloom seeds come from open-pollinated varieties that have been passed down for at least 50 years.
Hybrid seeds (F1) are the first generation cross between two parent varieties, bred for specific desirable traits.
Saving seeds from open-pollinated plants lets gardeners preserve favorite varieties and adapt plants to local conditions over time.
The world's largest flower, Rafflesia arnoldii, can measure over 3 feet across and smells like rotting flesh to attract pollinators.
Bamboo is the fastest-growing plant in the world, with some species growing up to 35 inches per day.
The corpse flower (Amorphophallus titanum) blooms rarely, sometimes waiting 7 to 10 years between flowers that last only 24 to 48 hours.
Japanese maples are prized ornamental trees that come in hundreds of cultivars with varied leaf shapes and colors.
Crepe myrtles bloom for up to 100 days, earning them the nickname 'the lilac of the South.'
Boxwood shrubs have been used in formal gardens for over 2,000 years, prized for their dense, shapeable growth.
Dogwood trees are among the first flowering trees in spring and provide important berries for migrating birds in fall.
Fruit trees like apples and pears require a certain number of 'chill hours' below 45°F to produce fruit.
Dwarf fruit tree varieties can produce full-sized fruit in containers or small yards with as little as 6 feet of space.
Espaliered fruit trees trained flat against walls were common in medieval monastery gardens.
Grapes have been cultivated for wine, juice, and fresh eating for over 8,000 years.
The oldest living tree in the world is a bristlecone pine named Methuselah, estimated to be over 4,800 years old.
Bonsai is the Japanese art of growing miniature trees in containers, with some specimens over 800 years old.
Topiary, the art of shaping plants into ornamental forms, dates back to Roman gardens in the first century AD.
Peonies can live for over 100 years and are often passed down through generations of gardening families.
Gladiolus bulbs should be dug up in fall in cold climates and stored indoors to replant in spring.
Dahlias come in an astonishing range of sizes, from 2-inch pompons to 12-inch dinner plate varieties.
Iris rhizomes should be planted with their tops exposed at the soil surface; burying them too deep prevents blooming.
Wisteria can take 3 to 5 years to bloom after planting and may need root pruning to encourage flowering.
Sweet peas are annual climbing flowers that prefer cool weather and should be planted as early in spring as the soil can be worked.
Zinnias are one of the easiest annual flowers to grow from seed and attract butterflies with their bright colors.
Marigolds release chemicals from their roots that repel root-knot nematodes in the soil.
Cosmos flowers thrive in poor soil and actually produce more blooms when not fertilized.
Morning glories are fast-growing annual vines that can climb 10 to 15 feet and reseed themselves prolifically.
The garden pea was the plant Gregor Mendel used to discover the basic laws of genetics in the 1860s.
Thomas Jefferson meticulously documented his gardening experiments at Monticello, growing over 300 vegetable varieties.
Victory gardens were revived by home gardeners during the COVID-19 pandemic as interest in growing food surged.
The word 'garden' comes from the Old English 'geard,' meaning an enclosed yard or piece of ground.
The Wardian case, invented in 1829, was a sealed glass container that allowed plants to be transported on long sea voyages.
The Dust Bowl of the 1930s demonstrated the devastating consequences of poor soil management and led to modern soil conservation practices.
Community gardens provide shared growing space and have been shown to improve nutrition, mental health, and neighborhood connections.
School gardens are increasingly used as outdoor classrooms to teach children about science, nutrition, and environmental stewardship.
Guerrilla gardening is the practice of cultivating plants on neglected or abandoned land without formal permission.
The Slow Food movement, founded in Italy in 1986, promotes local food gardens and traditional agricultural practices.
Rooftop gardens in cities reduce the urban heat island effect, manage stormwater, and provide habitat for pollinators.
Green walls or living walls use vertical panels of plants to improve air quality and aesthetics on building facades.
The average American lawn uses about 10,000 gallons of water per year beyond what rainfall provides.
Replacing traditional lawn with native groundcovers, moss, or clover reduces water, fertilizer, and mowing needs.
Moon gardening follows lunar phases, with planting above-ground crops during waxing moons and root crops during waning moons.
Succession sowing radishes every two weeks ensures a continuous supply of this quick-growing root vegetable.
Winter sowing involves planting seeds in mini greenhouses made from recycled containers and leaving them outdoors to germinate naturally.
Garden row covers made of spun polyester protect crops from frost and can extend the season by 4 to 6 weeks.
Cold frames are simple unheated structures with transparent tops that capture solar warmth to grow plants in cool weather.
Hot beds are cold frames heated from below by decomposing manure, used since the 17th century to start plants early.
Season extension techniques can allow gardeners in northern climates to harvest fresh greens even in December and January.
Garlic scapes, the curly flower stalks of hardneck garlic, are a delicious culinary byproduct harvested in early summer.
Edible flowers like nasturtiums, pansies, and violets add color and flavor to salads and desserts.
Microgreens are nutrient-dense seedlings harvested 7 to 14 days after germination and can be grown indoors year-round.
Sprouting seeds in jars requires no soil or light and produces nutritious sprouts in just 3 to 5 days.
Growing your own food can save hundreds of dollars per year and ensures access to the freshest produce possible.
